title: Te Whakatau Tohu: Navigating the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with Geometry and Measurement / Te Arotake Tohu: Te Whakatere i te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will explore the geometry and measurement of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway flowing through our rohe. They will learn how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors used sophisticated geometric knowledge to navigate, fish, and maintain this awa, understanding its flow patterns, depth variations, and the sacred geometry embedded in traditional pā sites along its banks.
learningObjectives: Accurately measure and calculate the perimeter and area of traditional fishing traps (pā tuna) used in the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Apply geometric principles to create scale maps of the stream's course using both Cartesian coordinates and traditional Māori directional references (e.g., Mataki, Tonga, Hauāuru, Raki)
Analyze water flow rates and volume calculations to understand traditional flood prediction methods used by Ngāti Kahungunu
Design and present a culturally-informed solution for a current environmental challenge facing the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using mathematical reasoning

activities.title: Te Arotake Awa: Stream Measurement Mahi
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to conduct hands-on measurements of width, depth, and flow rate at three designated points. They'll use traditional methods (tautau and pacing) alongside modern tools, recording data for later analysis and comparing the accuracy of both approaches.

activities.title: Whakamahere Whenua: Mapping with Dual Systems
activities.description: Using their field data, students create detailed scale maps of the stream section. They'll plot points using both Cartesian coordinates and traditional Māori directional systems, incorporating significant cultural landmarks like pā sites, traditional crossing points, and food gathering areas identified through local knowledge.

activities.title: Te Pūtaiao o te Pā Tuna: Geometry of Eel Traps
activities.description: Students examine traditional pā tuna designs from the Mangakōtukutuku area, calculating perimeter, area, and volume capacities. They'll then design their own pā tuna using geometric principles, ensuring their design meets specific measurement requirements for sustainable fishing practices.

activities.title: Hei Whakaro Māori: Cultural Mathematics Problem-Solving
activities.description: Working with local kaumātua (via virtual visit), students tackle real-world problems: calculating water volume needed to sustain native species, determining optimal angles for fish passage structures, and using geometric patterns to create culturally appropriate signage for stream restoration projects.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith (local knowledge expert available for virtual visit)
Mangakōtukutuku Stream Field Measurement Kit (measuring tapes, flow meters, GPS devices)
'Te Mātauranga o Ngāti Kahungunu' digital resource from Eastern Institute of Technology
NZ Maths Geometry and Measurement Level 4 resource materials with Māori contexts

tasks: Create a scaled map of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream section showing both Cartesian and traditional Māori coordinates
Produce calculations for a proposed pā tuna design including perimeter, area, and volume measurements
Write a mathematical report analyzing water flow data and proposing a culturally-informed solution for stream maintenance
Present findings to peers using both mathematical terminology and te reo Māori concepts

nzcAlignment: Geometry and Measurement: Level 4 - Use appropriate scales, devices, and metric units for length, area, volume and capacity, weight (mass), temperature, angle, and time
Geometry and Measurement: Level 4 - Use side or edge lengths to find the perimeters and areas of rectangles, parallelograms, and triangles and the volumes of cuboids
Number and Algebra: Level 4 - Generalise that whole numbers can be partitioned in many ways and that some partitions are more useful than others for particular calculations
Statistics: Level 4 - Evaluate the effectiveness of different displays in representing the findings of a statistical investigation or probability activity undertaken by others
